questions.question: What is the fundamental unit of a Temporal Application?

choices.text: An Activity, which performs the actual work and can be retried independently
A Worker, which polls for tasks and executes code on behalf of the server
A Workflow, which defines the durable, orchestration logic of the application
A Task Queue, which routes work to the appropriate registered handlers

questions.explanation: Workflows are the fundamental unit of a Temporal Application. They define the durable orchestration logic — the 'what and when' of your application. Unlike a plain function or a cron job, a Workflow's state survives process restarts and deployments, making long-running business processes reliable by default.

questions.question: When would you use the Workflow Reset feature?

choices.text: To pause a running Workflow and resume it from the beginning with updated input parameters
To restart a Workflow from a specific point in its history after fixing a bug, such as removing non-deterministic code that blocked it
To copy a Workflow's history to a new Namespace so it can continue running under different access controls
To roll back all completed Activities to their initial state so they can be re-executed with fresh data

questions.explanation: Reset is used when a Workflow is blocked — typically due to a non-determinism error introduced by a code change. After fixing the underlying bug, you reset the Workflow to a point before the problem in its event history. The new execution replays history up to that point and then runs forward with the corrected code. Progress after the reset point is discarded.

questions.question: What is a Dynamic Workflow in Temporal, and when does it get invoked?

choices.text: A Workflow that scales its concurrency dynamically based on current Worker load and available Task Queue capacity
A Workflow that is invoked at runtime when no other registered Workflow matches the requested Workflow name
A Workflow that automatically restarts itself with new parameters whenever its Schedule fires on a recurring basis
A Workflow that can change its own registered name while it is still running to handle routing updates

questions.explanation: A Dynamic Workflow acts as a catch-all handler. If a Workflow Execution is started with a name that doesn't match any specifically registered Workflow on the Worker, Temporal routes it to the Dynamic Workflow instead. Only one Dynamic Workflow can be registered per Worker, and it must accept raw input values to handle the unknown payload types.

questions.question: When should you use `Workflow.ResetAsync` (or the `temporal workflow reset` CLI command) instead of simply redeploying fixed code and letting the Workflow retry?

choices.text: Reset is used whenever you want to add new Signal or Query handlers to a running Workflow, since existing executions don't pick up new handlers without a reset.

Reset is used when a Workflow is blocked due to a non-determinism error or other issue that prevents it from progressing, and you need it to resume from an earlier Event History point with the fixed code.
Reset should be used after every new deployment to ensure all running Workflows start fresh with the latest code, discarding any accumulated Event History.
Reset is equivalent to cancellation followed by a new start, so it is used whenever you want to rerun a Workflow from the beginning without retaining any prior history.

questions.explanation: A Workflow Reset terminates the current execution and starts a new one from a specified point in the Event History, copying history up to that point. It is specifically designed for situations where a Workflow is stuck — typically due to non-deterministic code that was deployed — and needs to resume with corrected logic. Option A is wrong; Signals/Queries don't require a reset. Option C is wrong and dangerous — resetting every Workflow on deploy would discard all progress. Option D is wrong; reset preserves history up to the reset point, which is fundamentally different from a fresh start.
